original_abstract: Cyber-physical systems (CPS) serve as the backbone of modern infrastructure, yet their operational safety is frequently jeopardized by technical faults and anomalies. This study investigates the efficacy of open-source Large Language Models (LLMs)—including Mistral 7B, Llama 3.1 (8B), and Gemma 2—in detecting anomalies across two distinct domains: battery management and powertrain systems. We propose a specialized Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) framework featuring a novel two-step workflow: the models first infer baseline operational rules from normal behavioral data and subsequently apply those rules to identify deviations. Our experiments revealed that while standard prompting was effective for battery data, the more complex powertrain dataset required an optimized approach. By refining the prompt to emphasize rule inference, we achieved a significant boost in diagnostic accuracy. Results demonstrate that Mistral 7B produced F1-scores of up to 0.99, while Llama 3.1 and Gemma 2 achieved perfect 1.0 F1-scores in challenging scenarios. These findings highlight the critical role of prompt engineering and structured rule inference in leveraging LLMs to bolster the resilience of cyber-physical infrastructure.
humanized_abstract: Cyber-physical systems (CPSs) form the core of today’s critical infrastructure, and a multitude of failures occurring on a daily basis pose a serious threat to the operational safety of these systems due to faults and anomalies. This work explores the applicability of open-source Large Language Models (LLMs) — namely Mistral 7B, Llama 3.1 (8B), and Gemma 2 — on the anomaly detection task in the battery management and powertrain domains. Furthermore, a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) based approach is proposed, utilizing a two-stage method that extracts the underlying operational rules based on the provided fault-free system data and then applies them for anomaly detection. The achieved experimental results confirm that the standard prompt works well on battery data, but only leads to limited success with the powertrain data. An adjusted version of the prompt — which is constructed by introducing a rule-focused keyword — ultimately enhances the performance of the models. The best F1-score values are 0.99 achieved with Mistral 7B, and 1.0 that are reached by Llama 3.1 and Gemma 2 for specific cases. Overall, our experimental results demonstrate that optimal prompting and structured rule extraction are crucial for the applicability of LLMs in enhancing the reliability of the CPSs.
